description: Stable course topic and module definitions that can be reused across semesters even when the schedule changes.

modules.title: What Is Intelligence?
modules.unitFocus: Students begin by asking what counts as intelligence, who gets to define it, and how measurement, categorization, and data production shape public life.
modules.braidElsiArc: This unit introduces BRAID as a socio-technical system and establishes that categories such as normal, signal, noise, and anomaly have ethical and governance consequences.

meetings.title: Course launch: human + machine intelligence as public issue
meetings.focus: Course launch and human + machine intelligence as a public issue.
meetings.braidElsiConnection: AI is not just software; it is infrastructure, labor, hardware, institutions, and governance.

meetings.title: What counts as intelligence? Human, animal, machine, social, embodied
meetings.focus: What counts as intelligence across human, animal, machine, social, and embodied forms.
meetings.braidElsiConnection: Map BRAID as a socio-technical system involving chip, lab, funder, user, regulator, and public.

meetings.title: Measurement, categories, and intelligence
meetings.focus: Measurement, categories, and intelligence.
meetings.braidElsiConnection: Normal, signal, noise, and anomaly are categories with consequences.

meetings.title: Objectivity, neutrality, and who gets defined
meetings.focus: Objectivity, neutrality, and who gets defined.
meetings.braidElsiConnection: Who gets to define intelligence, normality, risk, or error?

meetings.title: Human learning, memory, dreams, prediction, adaptation
meetings.focus: Human learning, memory, dreams, prediction, and adaptation.
meetings.braidElsiConnection: A bridge into The Dream Hotel and the question of what gets lost when inner life becomes data.

meetings.title: Embodiment, subjectivity, and difficult-to-measure experience
meetings.focus: Embodiment, subjectivity, and difficult-to-measure experience.
meetings.braidElsiConnection: A strong opening for The Dream Hotel and its treatment of intimate, difficult-to-measure experience.

modules.title: How Do Machines Learn?
modules.unitFocus: Students learn how models, data, scores, and thresholds operate while also asking how those choices encode institutional priorities and historical assumptions.
modules.braidElsiArc: This unit prepares students to see that technical choices in sensing, feature design, thresholds, and adaptation shape downstream governance questions.

meetings.title: Data, rules, models, training, and inference
meetings.focus: Data, rules, models, training, and inference.
meetings.braidElsiConnection: Sensor choices, features, and spike encodings shape what hardware can see.

meetings.title: Generative AI as one paradigm, not all AI
meetings.focus: Generative AI as one paradigm, not all AI.
meetings.braidElsiConnection: Prevent overgeneralizing from LLMs to all AI, especially neuromorphic AI.

meetings.title: Supervised learning, scores, and decisions
meetings.focus: Supervised learning, scores, and decisions.
meetings.braidElsiConnection: False positives and false negatives matter differently across use cases.

meetings.title: Error, responsibility, and decision thresholds
meetings.focus: Error, responsibility, and decision thresholds.
meetings.braidElsiConnection: Connect thresholds to accountability, auditability, and oversight.

meetings.title: Prediction and historical data
meetings.focus: Prediction and historical data.
meetings.braidElsiConnection: Prior patterns become embedded in future anomaly judgments.

meetings.title: Feedback loops and neutral prediction
meetings.focus: Feedback loops and neutral prediction.
meetings.braidElsiConnection: Adaptive systems may learn from their own classifications.

modules.title: Brain-Inspired Computing and Neuromorphic Systems
modules.unitFocus: Students build technical grounding in neuroscience metaphors, neural networks, spiking systems, timing, and neuromorphic architectures without collapsing those systems into myths of human-like intelligence.
modules.braidElsiArc: The unit prepares students to analyze how brain metaphors, material substrates, and hardware design choices affect public understanding, trust, and governance.

meetings.title: Neuroscience primer: neurons, synapses, weights, plasticity
meetings.focus: Neuroscience primer covering neurons, synapses, weights, and plasticity.
meetings.braidElsiConnection: Ground biological inspiration before students critique neural networks.

meetings.title: Neural networks, opacity, trust, contestability, dependency
meetings.focus: Neural networks, opacity, trust, contestability, and dependency.
meetings.braidElsiConnection: Brain metaphors can mislead, and opaque systems can encourage over-trust or deference.

meetings.title: Fall Break
meetings.focus: Fall Break.
meetings.braidElsiConnection: No class.

meetings.title: Neuromorphic computing: SNNs, spikes, timing
meetings.focus: Neuromorphic computing through SNNs, spikes, and timing.
meetings.braidElsiConnection: Introduce spikes, timing, event-driven computation, and the limits of biological analogy.

modules.title: From Mechanism to Implications
modules.unitFocus: Students move from mechanism to consequence by connecting anomaly detection, sensing, edge AI, hardware, environmental claims, and institutional power to real governance questions.
modules.braidElsiArc: This is the core translational unit for turning neuromorphic mechanisms into questions about surveillance, accountability, vendor power, materials, and lifecycle harm.

meetings.title: Unsupervised learning, clustering, anomaly detection
meetings.focus: Unsupervised learning, clustering, and anomaly detection.
meetings.braidElsiConnection: Core BRAID concept: anomaly detection requires a baseline of expected behavior.

meetings.title: Normality, anomaly, surveillance
meetings.focus: Normality, anomaly, and surveillance.
meetings.braidElsiConnection: Misuse risk: anomaly detection can become surveillance or social control.

meetings.title: Sensors, edge AI, wearables, biosensing, cybersecurity
meetings.focus: Sensors, edge AI, wearables, biosensing, and cybersecurity.
meetings.braidElsiConnection: Edge or on-chip AI changes data flows, logging, oversight, and responsibility.

meetings.title: Edge AI, cloud, chips, vendors, human judgment
meetings.focus: Edge AI, cloud, chips, vendors, and human judgment.
meetings.braidElsiConnection: Hardware, vendors, standards, and repair shape who can govern or contest systems.

meetings.title: Hardware, memristors, energy, and material AI
meetings.focus: Hardware, memristors, energy, and material AI.
meetings.braidElsiConnection: Memristors and memtransistors, on-chip learning, energy efficiency, and lifecycle impacts.

meetings.title: BRAID neuromorphic anomaly detection
meetings.focus: BRAID neuromorphic anomaly detection.
meetings.braidElsiConnection: Apply NIST, EU AI Act, IEEE, and OECD-style questions to BRAID.

meetings.title: Explainability, adaptive hardware, and public understanding
meetings.focus: Explainability, adaptive hardware, and public understanding.
meetings.braidElsiConnection: Students translate a technical mechanism into public-facing language.

meetings.title: Environmental claims, efficiency, lifecycle costs
meetings.focus: Environmental claims, efficiency, and lifecycle costs.
meetings.braidElsiConnection: Efficiency claims must be weighed against materials, fabrication, e-waste, and scale.

modules.title: Governing Uncertain Futures
modules.unitFocus: Students connect stakeholders, harms, benefits, history, alternatives, and governance frameworks in order to draft public-facing analyses and proposals.
modules.braidElsiArc: The central question becomes how to govern anomaly detection and other emerging AI systems before their assumptions, harms, and dependencies become locked in.

meetings.title: Stakeholders, harms, benefits, governance
meetings.focus: Stakeholders, harms, benefits, and governance.
meetings.braidElsiConnection: Build an ELSI risk memo around a neuromorphic or emerging AI use case.

meetings.title: Alternatives, refusal, repair, democratic governance
meetings.focus: Alternatives, refusal, repair, and democratic governance.
meetings.braidElsiConnection: Ask when anomaly detection should be limited, redesigned, refused, or governed differently.

meetings.title: University Senior Symposium
meetings.focus: University Senior Symposium.
meetings.braidElsiConnection: No class.

meetings.title: Governance frameworks: NIST, EU AI Act, OECD, IEEE
meetings.focus: Governance frameworks including NIST, the EU AI Act, OECD, and IEEE.
meetings.braidElsiConnection: Turn technical and ethical analysis into governance questions.

modules.title: Public Communication and Final Synthesis
modules.unitFocus: Students revise public-facing work, practice peer feedback, and bring technical explanation into direct conversation with stakeholder analysis and governance reasoning.
modules.braidElsiArc: The culminating task is to connect mechanism, stakeholder concern, risk, and governance question in a form that is public-facing and usable.

meetings.title: Draft presentations / peer review
meetings.focus: Draft presentations and peer review.
meetings.braidElsiConnection: Review for technical accuracy, ELSI clarity, and public usefulness.

meetings.title: Thanksgiving Break
meetings.focus: Thanksgiving Break.
meetings.braidElsiConnection: No class.

meetings.title: Last day of class: synthesis
meetings.focus: Last day of class synthesis.
meetings.braidElsiConnection: Technical mechanism to stakeholder concern to risk to governance question.

meetings.title: Optional support
meetings.focus: Optional support.
meetings.braidElsiConnection: Optional final ELSI project support.

modules.title: Final Exam / Presentations
modules.unitFocus: Final presentations synthesize technical explanation, public communication, and governance analysis.

meetings.title: Final presentations
meetings.focus: Final presentations.
meetings.braidElsiConnection: Public-facing explainer plus ELSI risk analysis.
